(1) I was 10 the year my cousin Marley's parents gave her a painful Christmas surprise: they were getting a divorce .
(2) My aunt went to California, but my uncle decided to get her back.
(3) There was one matter: where to put his young daughter.
(4) Luckily, my mother loved Marley, giving her more attention than her family ever did.
(5) On that Christmas Eve, my cousin arrived on our doorstep carrying an old blue suitcase.
(6) Before she stepped inside, Marley said, "It doesn't matter.
(7) When my parents come back, we'll have a bigger Christmas than this."
(8) Late on the night Marley arrived, my mom came into my room, her arms filled with packages.
(9) I knew they were my Christmas presents.
(10) "I know how you love surprises but we have to decide which ones to give to Marley."
(11) My mother carefully opened the gifts.
(12) Wow!
(13) I saw ice skates, red leather gloves and a dollhouse.
(14) The last present was the music box we'd asked the lady at the local store to take down and play for us over and over again.
(15) "Which ones?"
(16) my mother asked.
(17) "She can have the ice skates," I said reluctantly .
(18) "That's good," my mother said.
(19) She began to tape the packages up again.
(20) Then she stopped and asked, "Are you sure about these?"
(21) At that moment, I realized she expected more from me than I had already given.
(22) So I gave her the music box, too, and smiled.
(23) The next morning, Marley's eyes were resolute .
(24) She expected nothing and wanted us to know she didn't care.
(25) But when she saw her name on the biggest box, she couldn't hide her excitement.
(26) I thought my happiness would be cut in half.
(27) Instead it was doubled.
(28) Over the last 40 years, Marley and I have grown apart .
(29) But the love we felt for each other that day still remains.
(30) Today Marley says, "They were the best presents I ever got.

Your task is to identify the evidence scope level among the following categories:

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

- - -

### Example

Passage: 
(1) "Can I see my baby?" asked the happy new mother.
(2) When the baby was in her arms, she couldn't believe her eyes.
(3) The baby was born without ears.
(4) Time proved  that the baby's hearing was very good though he had no ears.
(5) He got on well with his classmates.
(6) But one day, he said to his mother, tears in his eyes, "A big boy called me a freak  ."  

Not True Statement: The boy couldn't hear without ears at first.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Understand the Statement
   - Statement: "The boy couldn't hear without ears at first."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: The boy was initially unable to hear due to being born without ears.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (3): States the baby was born without ears.
   - Sentence (4): States that his hearing was very good despite that.

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

---

### Input

Passage: 
(1) It takes a lot of skills and hard work to set a world record in pumpkin-carving .
(2) Steve Clarke, a sixth-grade teacher from Pennsylvania, is a famous carver .
(3) Clarke has set many world records for carving pumpkins quickly.
(4) When he competes, he must cut our two eyes, two eyebrows, a nose, a mouth, and two ears as fast as possible.
(5) But Clarke knows something else about pumpkin-carving.
(6) It's easier to get a record than to keep it.
(7) He took the title in 2000 when he carved a pumpkin face in 74.8 seconds.
(8) That time was 19 seconds faster than what Jerry had set.
(9) From then on, Clarke broke his own records five times.
(10) In 2006, he set his best time in Florida.
(11) His winning time was 24.03 seconds!
(12) The only problem is that someone finally beat that mark.
(13) On October 7, 2010, David set the new record with 20.1 seconds.
(14) Clarke was not able to break that record this year, but he has planned to break the record.
(15) After Halloween, he will go to local pumpkin farms to practise designs and new skills.
(16) Clarke is popular because of his super pumpkin-carving skills.
(17) In 1999, he carved more than 30 pumpkins at the Halloween party for then vice-president Al Gore.  

True Statement: Clarke is from Pennsylvania.  

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. **Understand the Statement**
   - Statement: "Clarke is from Pennsylvania."
   - Label: True
   - Key claim: Clarke's residence is in Pennsylvania.

2. **Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage**
   - Sentence (2): "Steve Clarke, a sixth-grade teacher from Pennsylvania, is a famous carver."

3. **Assess Scope of Evidence**
   - Sentence (2) explicitly states that Steve Clarke is from Pennsylvania.
   - This sentence contains all the necessary information to determine the truth of the statement.
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

Your task is to identify the evidence scope level among the following categories:

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

- - -

### Example

Passage: 
(1) "Can I see my baby?" asked the happy new mother.
(2) When the baby was in her arms, she couldn't believe her eyes.
(3) The baby was born without ears.
(4) Time proved  that the baby's hearing was very good though he had no ears.
(5) He got on well with his classmates.
(6) But one day, he said to his mother, tears in his eyes, "A big boy called me a freak  ."  

Not True Statement: The boy couldn't hear without ears at first.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Understand the Statement
   - Statement: "The boy couldn't hear without ears at first."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: The boy was initially unable to hear due to being born without ears.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (3): States the baby was born without ears.
   - Sentence (4): States that his hearing was very good despite that.

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

---

### Input

Passage: 
(1) Han Bin, a 15-year-old Chinese boy, likes eating snacks very much.
(2) He prefers cream   biscuits, McDonald's food and Beijing roast duck.
(3) Sometimes he eats two packages of biscuits a day.
(4) As a result, he becomes fatter and fatter.
(5) Today in China, there are many children like Han Bin.
(6) They like snacks.
(7) They don't eat enough healthy food like vegetables and fruit.
(8) It's bad for their health.
(9) Bad eating habit is a serious   problem.
(10) Health experts   say that children should have healthy eating habits.
(11) First, they must eat regular meals and have a good breakfast.
(12) Then, they should have different kinds of foods, such as fruit, vegetables, eggs, milk, meat and so on.
(13) Third, children shouldn't eat too many snacks and too much fried food.
(14) They are delicious, but eating too much of them is unhealthy.
(15) If children do as the experts say, they won't become fat.
(16) They will be healthier.
(17) And they will have enough energy to study well.  

True Statement: Health experts say that children must have a good breakfast.  

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. **Understand the Statement**
   - Statement: "Health experts say that children must have a good breakfast."
   - Label: True
   - Key claim: Health experts recommend that children should have a good breakfast.

2. **Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage**
   - Sentence (10): "Health experts say that children should have healthy eating habits."
   - Sentence (11): "First, they must eat regular meals and have a good breakfast."

3. **Assess Scope of Evidence**
   - Sentence (11) directly states that health experts recommend having a good breakfast.
   - Sentence (10) introduces the subject "Health experts" and their advice on healthy eating habits.
   - Sentence (11) refers to "they" which clearly refers to "Health experts" from sentence (10).
   - Therefore, both sentences are needed to fully understand and verify the statement.

Answer: Inter

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).

(7) A home is more than a roof and four walls.
(8) It's the cooking, eating, talking, playing and family living that go on inside, which are important as well.
(9) And at home you usually feel safe and relaxed.
(10) But it's not just that homes look different in different countries, they also contain different things and reveal  different attitudes and needs.
(11) For example, in cold northern Europe, there's a fire in the living room or kitchen and all the chairs face it.
(12) In the south, where the sun shines a lot and it's more important to keep the heat out, there are small windows, cool stone floors and often no carpets.
(13) We asked some people about their homes.

(15) "In my country many people don't stay in one place for a very long time.
(16) They often move every ten years or so."

(18) What are typical features of homes in your country?
(19) "In Britain, even in town there's always a garden and sometimes a cellar .

You are given a passage and a statement labeled as either True or Not True based on the content of the passage. 
Your task is to identify the evidence scope level among the following categories:

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

- - -

### Example

Passage: 
(1) "Can I see my baby?" asked the happy new mother.
(2) When the baby was in her arms, she couldn't believe her eyes.
(3) The baby was born without ears.
(4) Time proved  that the baby's hearing was very good though he had no ears.
(5) He got on well with his classmates.
(6) But one day, he said to his mother, tears in his eyes, "A big boy called me a freak  ."  

Not True Statement: The boy couldn't hear without ears at first.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Understand the Statement
   - Statement: "The boy couldn't hear without ears at first."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: The boy was initially unable to hear due to being born without ears.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (3): States the baby was born without ears.
   - Sentence (4): States that his hearing was very good despite that.

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

---

### Input

Passage: 
(1) What does the word "home" mean to you?
(2) How do you say the word in French?
(3) In Spanish?
(4) In your language?
(5) Although people usually know what the word means, it often has no exact translation.
(6) It's not surprising really, because the idea of home differs from country to country, and from person to person.
(7) A home is more than a roof and four walls.
(8) It's the cooking, eating, talking, playing and family living that go on inside, which are important as well.
(9) And at home you usually feel safe and relaxed.
(10) But it's not just that homes look different in different countries, they also contain different things and reveal  different attitudes and needs.
(11) For example, in cold northern Europe, there's a fire in the living room or kitchen and all the chairs face it.
(12) In the south, where the sun shines a lot and it's more important to keep the heat out, there are small windows, cool stone floors and often no carpets.
(13) We asked some people about their homes.
(14) How often do people move house in your country?
(15) "In my country many people don't stay in one place for a very long time.
(16) They often move every ten years or so."
(17) Cheryl, Boston, USA
(18) What are typical features of homes in your country?
(19) "In Britain, even in town there's always a garden and sometimes a cellar .
(20) We have separate bedrooms and living rooms.
(21) But we don't often have balconies  .
(22) The weather isn't warm enough!"
(23) -- Pat, Exeter, England  

True Statement: "Home" is a place where you feel safe and relaxed.  

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. **Understand the Statement**
   - Statement: "Home" is a place where you feel safe and relaxed.
   - Label: True
   - Key claim: A home is a place where one feels safe and relaxed.

2. **Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage**
   - Sentence (9): "And at home you usually feel safe and relaxed."

3. **Assess Scope of Evidence**
   - Sentence (9) directly states that at home, you usually feel safe and relaxed.
   - This sentence contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

(1) I was now in my twenty third year of residence in this island and was accustomed to the place and to the manner of living.
(2) If it had not been the savages who had come to the place to disturb me, I could have been content to spent the rest of my time there, even to the last moment, till I had laid me down and died, like the old goat in the cave.
(3) I had also arrived to some little recreations and amusements, which made the time pass more pleasantly with me a great deal than it did before.
(4) At first, I had taught my Poll to speak.
(5) And he did it so familiarly and talked so clearly and plain that it was very pleasant to me.
(6) And he lived with me no less than twenty six years.
(7) How long he might live afterwards, I don't know; though I know they have an idea in Brazil that they live a hundred years.
(8) Perhaps poor Poll may be alive there still, calling Poor Robin Crusoe to this day.
(9) I wish no other English man had the bad luck to go there and hear him.
(10) But if he did, he would certainly believe it was the devil .
(11) My dog was a very pleasant and loving companion to me, for no less than sixteen years of my time, and then died of mere old age.
(12) As for my cats, they multiplied to that degree that I had to shoot several of them at first to keep them from eating up all I had.
(13) Besides these, I had two more parrots which talked pretty well and would all call Robin Crusoe, but none like my first.
(14) Nor indeed did I take the pains with any of them that I had done with him.
(15) I had also several sea-fowls, whose names I don't know, that I caught upon the shore and cut their wings.
(16) And the little stakes which I had planted before my castle wall being now grown up to a good thick bush, these fowls all lived among these low trees and bred there, which was very agreeable to me; so that as I said above, I began to be well content with the life I led if not worried about the threat from the savages.

(2) If it had not been the savages who had come to the place to disturb me, I could have been content to spent the rest of my time there, even to the last moment, till I had laid me down and died, like the old goat in the cave.
(3) I had also arrived to some little recreations and amusements, which made the time pass more pleasantly with me a great deal than it did before.
(4) At first, I had taught my Poll to speak.
(5) And he did it so familiarly and talked so clearly and plain that it was very pleasant to me.
(6) And he lived with me no less than twenty six years.
(7) How long he might live afterwards, I don't know; though I know they have an idea in Brazil that they live a hundred years.
(8) Perhaps poor Poll may be alive there still, calling Poor Robin Crusoe to this day.
(9) I wish no other English man had the bad luck to go there and hear him.
(10) But if he did, he would certainly believe it was the devil .
(11) My dog was a very pleasant and loving companion to me, for no less than sixteen years of my time, and then died of mere old age.
(12) As for my cats, they multiplied to that degree that I had to shoot several of them at first to keep them from eating up all I had.
(13) Besides these, I had two more parrots which talked pretty well and would all call Robin Crusoe, but none like my first.
(14) Nor indeed did I take the pains with any of them that I had done with him.
(15) I had also several sea-fowls, whose names I don't know, that I caught upon the shore and cut their wings.
(16) And the little stakes which I had planted before my castle wall being now grown up to a good thick bush, these fowls all lived among these low trees and bred there, which was very agreeable to me; so that as I said above, I began to be well content with the life I led if not worried about the threat from the savages.  

True Statement: Parrots raised by Robin could talk very well.  

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. **Understand the Statement**
   - Statement: "Parrots raised by Robin could talk very well."
   - Label: True
   - Key claim: Parrots raised by Robin had good speaking abilities.

2. **Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage**
   - Sentence (4): Mentions that Robin taught his parrot, Poll, to speak.
   - Sentence (5): Describes Poll's ability to speak clearly and familiarly.
   - Sentence (13): Mentions that Robin had two more parrots which talked pretty well.

3. **Assess Scope of Evidence**
   - Sentence (5) explicitly states that Poll, one of the parrots, talked clearly and plainly.
   - Sentence (13) mentions that the other two parrots also talked pretty well.
   - Both sentences together provide a comprehensive view of the parrots' speaking abilities.
   - Since the statement refers to "parrots" in general and not just Poll, we need to consider both sentences (5) and (13) to fully support the claim.

Answer: Inter

2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.


3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.

(7) People from around the world travel to Piobbico to tell their sad stories of ugliness.

(11) They carry ID cards that grade their ugliness from bearable to extreme.

(13) The Ugly Club president insists that ugliness is a virtue.

(19) He believes that too many people suffer from financial and emotional pressures because they don't meet society's standards of beauty.

(1) When we say older people shrink, we don't mean they become tiny enough to fit in your pocket!
(2) We just mean that it's common for older people to become a little shorter over time.
(3) This kind of shrinking can't be reversed, although people can slow or stop this process.
(4) But why does shrinking happen at all?
(5) Because of gravity (the force that keeps your feet on the ground) , cushions between the bones in the spine  , get compressed  , which makes a person lose a little height and become shorter.
(6) Another reason why some older people shrink is because of osteoporosis  .
(7) Osteoporosis occurs when bone is broken down and not enough new bone material is made.
(8) Over time, bone is said to be lost because it's not being replaced.
(9) Bones become smaller and weaker and can easily break if someone with osteoporosis is injured.
(10) Older people--especially women, who generally have smaller and lighter bones to begin with--are more likely to develop osteoporosis.
(11) As years go by, a person with osteoporosis can get small breaks in bones that are called compression fractures .
(12) These breaks cause collapse of the spine and over time the person with osteoporosis can become hunched over  .
(13) Did you know that every day you shrink a little, too?
(14) You aren't as tall at the end of the day as you are at the beginning.
(15) That's because as the day goes on, water in the disks of the spine gets compressed due to gravity, making you just a tiny bit shorter.
(16) Don't worry, though.
(17) Once you get a good night's rest, your body recovers, and the next morning, you're standing tall again!
(18) Smoking and drinking alcohol can make this problem worse.
(19) Want to do something right now to build strong bones?
(20) I believe what I will say will help you.

(1) Most people consider skydiving   a product of the 20th century, but its history actually goes further back than that.
(2) The Chinese attempted to parachute   in the 10th century, a thousand years before we did.
(3) The Chinese did what we would today call base diving; that is, they jumped off a place that would allow them to float from a height to the ground.
(4) The first person to attempt to parachute was a Frenchman named Jacques Garnerin.
(5) He jumped from his hot-air balloon at the end of the 18th century and did tricks on the way down and amazed the crowds by landing safely on the ground.
(6) At the end of the 19th century, Kathie Paulus, a brave German woman, became famous for her skydiving skills.
(7) Once the airplane was invented, skydiving took on a whole new form.
(8) The airplane made it possible to dive from greater heights at greater speeds, allowing for more range in the movements in the air.
(9) A woman named Tiny Broadwick became the first woman to jump from a plane in 1913 and dive free fall in 1914.
(10) Skydiving was not called skydiving until the middle of the 1950s, when Ronald Young invented the word.
(11) It had been called parachuting before this and was mainly used by the military   to land troops in inland locations, or for pilots to jump out of their planes when necessary.
(12) Once World War I was over, parachuting became a sport, which we now call skydiving.
(13) After World War II, this activity became more a hobby than a military action.
(14) Soldiers were trained in parachuting and enjoyed the thrill so much that they continued on for fun.
(15) From this, teams and competitions were formed.
(16) Skydiving schools appeared in the late 1950s and now it is a recognized extreme sport enjoyed by many.

a|true|high20111.txt.passage:
(1) Where is that noise coming from?
(2) Not sure?
(3) Try living with your eyes closed for a few years.
(4) Blind people are better at locating sounds than people who can see, a new study says, without the benefits of vision the ears seem to work much better.
(5) Previous studies have shown that blind people are better than others at reaching out and touching the sources of sounds that are close by.
(6) Researchers from the University of Montreal wanted to see if blind people were also better at locating sounds that are far away.
(7) Twenty-three blind people participated in the study.
(8) All had been sightless for at least 20 years.
(9) Fourteen of them had lost their vision before age 11. the rest went blind after age 16.
(10) The experiment also included 10 people who could see but were wearing blind-folds.
(11) In one task, volunteers had to pick the direction of a sound coming from about 3 metres away.
(12) When the sound was in front of them or slightly off center in front, both groups performed equally well.
(13) When sounds came from the side or the back, however, the blind group performed much better than the blindfolded group.
(14) The participants who had been blind since childhood did slightly better than those who lost their sight later.
(15) Recognizing the locations of distant sounds can be a matter of life-or-death for blind people, say the researchers.
(16) Crossing the street, for instance, is much harder when you can't see the cars coming.
(17) Still, the researchers were surprised by how well the blind participants did, especially those who went blind after age 16.
(18) In another experiment, the scientists also found that parts of the brain that normally deal with visual information became active in locating sound in the people who were blind by age 11.
(19) These brain parts didn't show sound-location activity in the other group of blind people or in the sighted people.
(20) The scientists now want to learn more about the working of brains of "late-onset" blind people.

(1) Hundreds of students from around the world gathered in New York City last week for the Microsoft Imagine Cup finals.
(2) They came to present their ideas for using technology to solve world problems.
(3) Microsoft education director Suzi Levine says the nine-year-old program began mainly as a competition to create technology.
(4) SUZI LEVINE: "When we realized that students really actually want to have a purpose for what they're creating, we introduced the idea of inspiring them with the UN Millennium Development Goals and suggesting that they use those for their muse .
(5) "This past year we also rolled out something called the Imagine Cup Solve This library , where IGOs, NGOs and nonprofits can submit some of the technical challenges that they would like students to consider for their solutions."
(6) Microsoft says over 350,000 high school and college students registered for this year's competition.
(7) Judges chose more than four hundred of them to attend the finals.
(8) SUZI LEVINE: "One from Thailand was called NewKrean, where they created a Windows Phone 7 application that allows you to broadcast your location to your social network of friends so that you can be more easily rescued."
(9) They named their application Terra.
(10) Suzi Levine says there were also ideas from Egypt inspired by the revolution that removed president Hosni Mubarak in February.
(11) SUZI LEVINE: "One was to use Bluetooth as sort of a Twitter equivalent so that if the government shuts down the Internet, you actually can still have a massive social distribution."
(12) Students competed in nine categories.
(13) For example, in software design the top prize of twenty-five thousand dollars went to Team Hermes from Ireland.
(14) The students developed a device for cars to collect information on road conditions, driving behavior and traffic incidents.
(15) A team from Taiwan's National Tsing Hua University won first place in the embedded  development category.
(16) They developed a network of wireless devices to help plot the safest escape routes during a fire.
(17) Next year's awards ceremony will take place in Australia.
(18) Registration for Imagine Cup twenty-twelve opened Friday.
(19) Also, Microsoft announced plans for a three million dollar program to help Imagine Cup winners further develop their projects.

(1) Some kinds of mental skills naturally decrease as people get older.
(2) Yet research seems to show that some training can improve such skills.
(3) A recently published study also appears to demonstrate that the good effects of training can last for many years after that training has ended.
(4) Researchers at Johns Hopkins University in Maryland wanted to learn how long memory and thinking skills would last in older people who trained to keep them.

(6) They were taught methods meant to improve their memory, thinking and ability to perform everyday tasks.
(7) More than 2,800 volunteered for the study called ACTIVE - short for Advanced (Cognitive) Training for Independent and Vital Elderly.

(10) One class trained participants in skills including how to remember word lists.
(11) Another group trained in reasoning.

(13) A fourth group - the control group did not get any training.
(14) Earlier results had established that the training helped the participants for up to five years.

(17) "We are wondering whether those effects which endured over time would still be there ten years following the training, and in fact, that's exactly what we found."
(18) The effect on memory, however, seemed not to last as long.

(20) The total training time for the older people was between 10 and 15 hours.

(2) Yet research seems to show that some training can improve such skills.
(3) A recently published study also appears to demonstrate that the good effects of training can last for many years after that training has ended.
(4) Researchers at Johns Hopkins University in Maryland wanted to learn how long memory and thinking skills would last in older people who trained to keep them.
(5) The people were part of the ten-year research project.
(6) They were taught methods meant to improve their memory, thinking and ability to perform everyday tasks.
(7) More than 2,800 volunteered for the study called ACTIVE - short for Advanced (Cognitive) Training for Independent and Vital Elderly.
(8) Most studied when they were more than 70 years old.
(9) The volunteers took one of several short training classes meant to help them keep their mental abilities.
(10) One class trained participants in skills including how to remember word lists.
(11) Another group trained in reasoning.
(12) A third group received help with speed-of-processing - speed of receiving and understanding information.
(13) A fourth group - the control group did not get any training.
(14) Earlier results had established that the training helped the participants for up to five years.
(15) Now, lead study writer George Rebok says, the research showed most of the training remained effective a full ten years later.
(16) Professor Rebok and his team found that the people trained in reasoning and speed-of-processing did better on tests than the control group.
(17) "We are wondering whether those effects which endured over time would still be there ten years following the training, and in fact, that's exactly what we found."
(18) The effect on memory, however, seemed not to last as long.
(19) Still, the old people in any of the three classes generally reported less difficulty in performing daily activities than the control group.
(20) The total training time for the older people was between 10 and 15 hours.
d2|true|high6364.txt.statement: The second group were trained how to remember word lists.

Your task is to identify the evidence scope level among the following categories:

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

- - -

### Example

Passage: 
(1) "Can I see my baby?" asked the happy new mother.
(2) When the baby was in her arms, she couldn't believe her eyes.
(3) The baby was born without ears.
(4) Time proved  that the baby's hearing was very good though he had no ears.
(5) He got on well with his classmates.
(6) But one day, he said to his mother, tears in his eyes, "A big boy called me a freak  ."  

Not True Statement: The boy couldn't hear without ears at first.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Understand the Statement
   - Statement: "The boy couldn't hear without ears at first."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: The boy was initially unable to hear due to being born without ears.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (3): States the baby was born without ears.
   - Sentence (4): States that his hearing was very good despite that.

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

(11) In 2004, American microbiologist Jeffrey Gordon and his colleagues discovered a general link between obesity and gut microbiota in mice.
(12) While a link was believed to exist, proving it was another matter.
(13) "The list of diseases that they may play a role in is just growing and growing," says Lita Proctor, director of the US National Institutes of Health.
(14) "But the problem is that we're only able to look at associations and aren't yet able to conduct cause-and-effect studies."
(15) In the clinical study, researchers found a growth of too much endotoxin-producing bacteria, leading t0 35 percent ofthe gut bacteria, in an obese patient whose initial weight was 175 kg.
(16) Based on this information, researchers tried to cure the patient by feeding him a specialized nutritional liquid food to decrease the bacteria in his guts to ignorable amounts.
(17) After 23 weeks, the patient lost 51.4 kg, with his fatty liver disease having almost disappeared.

(1) If you like bicycling as much as you like playing ball and can't seem to decide between the two sports, you may want to consider cycle ball--a fun sport that adopts the key attributes from both activities and transforms it into a totally new game.
(2) Also known as red ball, the game was invented in 1893 by German laborers who wished to play polo , but could not afford horses.
(3) While the sport has not caught on in North America, it is very fashionable in Europe and even Japan.
(4) Played in an indoor court, cycle ball is similar to soccer in many ways--the aim is to score as many goals as possible, and only the goalkeeper is allowed to touch the ball.
(5) The one big difference?
(6) Use of legs is forbidden.
(7) Instead, players have to guide the ball across the court, using the front wheels of their bicycles or with their heads.
(8) In fact, if a player even touches the floor with his feet during the 14-minute game, it is considered a foul  and the opposing team gets a free kick.
(9) While the game was initially  played with normal bikes, things are a little different now.
(10) The seats of these specialized bikes are positioned over the back tire and they sport a single fixed gear, making it easier for the rider to control the constant back and forth movement required for the game.
(11) The handlebars  also point straight up so that the competitors can sit upright while playing and they are about twice the weight of normal bikes, which helps with the stability.
(12) While cycle ball may sound a little strange, it is a fast-paced sport that requires much training, skill, and, is also really fun to watch.

(13) But evolutionists have their own ideas.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.

- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.

(1) NOT all math skills are learned in the classroom.
(2) Some of them come naturally.
(3) Consider the split-second calculations  you make when you estimate   the number of empty seats on the school bus or the number of cookies in a cookie jar.
(4) These estimates can often be done without counting.
(5) That's because humans are born with the ability to closely guess the number of items in a group.
(6) Researchers call this ability a person's "number sense".
(7) To see how a person's inborn number sense might be linked to mathematical performance in the classroom, psychologist Justin Halberda and his colleagues from Johns Hopkins University in prefix = st1 /Baltimore,USran some tests.
(8) The scientists asked 64 14-year-olds to look at images   of yellow and blue dots that flashed on a computer screen for a fraction   of a second.
(9) Each image contained between 10 and 32 dots that varied in size.
(10) Some images contained twice as many blue dots  as yellow dots.
(11) In other images, however, the number of blue and yellow dots was nearly equal.
(12) For each image, the students were asked to estimate which color1 had more dots.
(13) The scientists found a wide variation in how well students could pick the color1 with the most dots.
(14) The scientists then looked at the students' math scores dating back to kindergarten.
(15) Children that performed best in the image test also scored the highest in standard math tests.
(16) The same finding held true at the other end of the spectrum  .
(17) Students who didn't score well on the image test received lower math scores.
(18) The study was the first to show a link between a person's inborn number sense and his or her achievement in formal math training.
(19) Does this connection mean that one cannot be good in math if they have a weak number sense?
(20) Or that having a strong number sense is a guarantee for good grades in math?
(21) The answers are not clear.
(22) While scientists continue looking at the possible links between a person's number sense and math achievement, one thing is certain: Doing lots of math homework will increase your chances of success.

(12) Simona's parents were able to ease the tension in the family.

You are given a passage and a statement labeled as either True or Not True based on the content of the passage. 
Your task is to identify the evidence scope level among the following categories:

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

- - -

### Example

Passage: 
(1) "Can I see my baby?" asked the happy new mother.
(2) When the baby was in her arms, she couldn't believe her eyes.
(3) The baby was born without ears.
(4) Time proved  that the baby's hearing was very good though he had no ears.
(5) He got on well with his classmates.
(6) But one day, he said to his mother, tears in his eyes, "A big boy called me a freak  ."  

Not True Statement: The boy couldn't hear without ears at first.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Understand the Statement
   - Statement: "The boy couldn't hear without ears at first."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: The boy was initially unable to hear due to being born without ears.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (3): States the baby was born without ears.
   - Sentence (4): States that his hearing was very good despite that.

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

---

### Input

Passage: 
(1) Are you a big fan of pirate  stories?
(2) Do you know how to talk like a pirate?
(3) You can have fun on September 19th.
(4) This special holiday is called International Talk Like A Pirate Day.
(5) It was invented in 1995 by two young Americans, John Baur and Mark Summers.
(6) These two good friends came up with this idea when they were playing racquetball, and began to talk like pirates.
(7) At that moment, they thought, "Young people are interested in pirate, but there is no such a chance for people to get together to share it.
(8) Why not have a day when everybody talks like a pirate?"
(9) They decided to make September 19th the date.
(10) Since then, Baur and Summers have tried hard to promote Talk Like A Pirate Day.
(11) As Baur said, "We've talked like pirates, and encouraged several of our friends to do so every September 19th."
(12) At first, this day was only known by a few people.
(13) In 2002, Baur and Summers sent a letter about their invented holiday to Dave Barry.
(14) This columnist really liked their idea.
(15) He promoted the holiday and made it known to more and more people.
(16) Now, it has really become an international holiday.
(17) On Talk Like A Pirate Day, you need to dress like a pirate.
(18) You should wear a big hat or maybe cover one of your eyes.
(19) You can pretend to be any kind of pirate that you can find in any story or movie.
(20) Having fun and being relaxed are not the only best things about the holiday.
(21) Young people share the same interest and childhood dreams that day.
(22) That's why it is welcomed by so many people, especially young people.
(23) Unlike traditional holidays, International Talk Like A Pirate Day is a brand new holiday which is invented by the youth themselves and celebrated by themselves too.
(24) Because of Baur and Summers' imagination and creativity, thousands of young people's pirate dream come true.
(25) Next time, when you have a good idea, why not share it with your fiends and insist on your belief?
(26) Maybe you can create another wonderful holiday for the youth in the future.  

True Statement: It was Dave Barry who made more people know the holiday.  

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. **Understand the Statement**
   - Statement: "It was Dave Barry who made more people know the holiday."
   - Label: True
   - Key claim: Dave Barry played a significant role in popularizing the holiday.

2. **Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage**
   - Sentence (13): Mentions that Baur and Summers sent a letter about their invented holiday to Dave Barry.
   - Sentence (14): States that Dave Barry really liked their idea.
   - Sentence (15): Indicates that Dave Barry promoted the holiday and made it known to more and more people.

3. **Assess Scope of Evidence**
   - Sentence (15) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is true.
   - It explicitly states that Dave Barry promoted the holiday and made it known to more people.
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

a|notTrue|high20138.txt.passage:
(1) How To Apply for USA Passports Online
(2) If you live in the United States, and you're planning a trip abroad, it is important to apply for new passports at least 2 months in advance (to avoid extra charges for an expedited passport processing).
(3) You can now get the application for a passport online.
(4) Some directions are detailed below:
(5) Downloading the passport application: The USA passport application, Form DS-11, is available for download on the US Department of State's website.
(6) *The passport application form is a Portable Document Format (PDF) file, and your computer must have Adobe Acrobat Reader to open it.
(7) If you do not have Adobe Acrobat Reader, you must first download the Adobe Acrobat software.
(8) You can do so by clicking here.
(9) *If you would like to download a blank application,      click here.
(10) If you would like to fill out the application online, then print it,click here.
(11) Printing the passport application: When printing the passport application, there are several requirements that must be followed.
(12) *You must use standard paper that is sized 8 1/2 inches by 11 inches.
(13) *The printing must be clear and legible.
(14) *The paper must be a standard medium weight, matte white (not shiny), and ink must be black.
(15) *The printer's ink must be black and there may not be any holes or perforations in the paper.
(16) *After printing, make sure that the box for pasting on your passport photo is the correct size of 2 inches by 2 inches.
(17) *It is important to print the application on one-sided paper.
(18) An application that is printed on double-sided paper will not be accepted.
(19) *For more information on these guidelines, visit the US Department of State's website.
(20) Filling out the passport application: As stated above, there are two ways to fill out the passport application: 1) typing your answers into the online form 2) writing answers by hand, in black ink, or by use of a typewriter.
(21) In both cases, you will need to provide the following information.
(22) *Your full name
(23) *Age
(24) *Date of birth
(25) *Place of birth
(26) *Phone number
(27) *Hair color1
(28) *Social security number
(29) *Mailing address 
(30) *Permanent Address.
(31) *Occupation
(32) *Employer
(33) *Your parents' names and birth information
(34) *Your spouse's name and birth information
(35) *Any other names you have used previously
(36) *Your travel plans
(37) *Emergency contact information
(38) Signing the application: It is important that you do not sign the application until you take the oath administered only by an authorized passport acceptance agent.
(39) Submitting the passport application: Along with the completed passport application, you must submit the following:
(40) *Proof of US Citizenship
(41) *Proof of Identity
(42) *Two recent passport sized (2x2 inches) photographs
(43) *The passport application fee

(1) When, after a year of being alone on his island, Robinson Crusoe sees a footprint in the sand, the reader of Robinson Crusoe trembles.
(2) Will Crusoe find another human being to end his loneliness?
(3) Is the footprint the sign of an enemy?
(4) Since 1719, when Daniel Defoe wrote Robinson Crusoe, thousands of people who enjoy English novels have thrilled to this great adventure story.
(5) But few know how the story came to be written.
(6) Robinson Crusoe was the first English novel.
(7) Its birth brought together the misadventures of a Scotch "failure" and the untapped imagination of an aging English scribbler.
(8) Near the end of the Seventeenth Century, the hot-tempered Alexander Selkirk was charged with bad conduct while in church.
(9) Rather than face this charge, he ran away to sea.
(10) Several years later, Selkirk found himself on the ship of an English privateer.
(11) The privateer was preying on Spanish shipping.
(12) But Selkirk quarreled bitterly with the Captain.
(13) So, when the ship came to the island of Juan Femandez in the South Seas, Selkirk asked to be put ashore.
(14) When he saw that there were no people on the island, he begged to be taken back on board.
(15) But the Captain refused--Selkirk had gone too far.
(16) Over four years later, Selkirk was rescued by another ship.
(17) When Selkirk got back to England, the story of his life on the island fired the imagination of Daniel Defoe.
(18) Defoe had been earning a living by his pen since he was thirty.
(19) He was amazingly hard-working.
(20) He wrote a whole newspaper three times a week.
(21) He also made part of his living from politics.
(22) He supported both political parties.
(23) He told each party that it had his sole support.
(24) Defoe's morals were weak.
(25) But he was a fine writer.
(26) He was almost sixty when, in the midst of his work in politics, he wrote Robinson Crusoe.
(27) In it, Defoe--said one critic--"forged  a story, and forced it on the world for truth."
(28) The detail of Crusoe's battle for survival on a lonely island is so vivid that the reader of Robinson Crusoe accepts the product of the author's imagination for reality.
(29) Robinson Crusoe became the first of a long line of heroes and heroines that have peopled English novels since Defoe's time.

a|true|college528.txt.passage:
(1) Sloth may be seen as a sin, but some of history's most accomplished men were fond of lounging around.
(2) Leonardo da Vinci enjoyed napping.
(3) So did Albert Einstein and Winston Churchill.
(4) Richard Buckminster Fuller advocated taking 30-minute naps every six hours.
(5) He is reported to have abandoned the practice only because" his schedule conflicted with that of his business associates, who insisted on sleeping like other men."
(6) No one has yet proved a correlation between napping and artistic brilliance or professional success, but an intriguing study published this week in the Archives of Internal Medicine claims to find a link between daytime siestas and good health.
(7) A team of researchers led by Androniki Naska of the University of Athens Medical School and Dimitrios Trichopoulos of Harvard's School of Public Health followed over 23,000 Greek patients with no history of coronary disease, cancer or stroke, for an average of six years.
(8) Their conclusion: napping just might save your life.
(9) The study found that the group of adults who took siestas (defined as 30-minute naps) at least three times a week had a third fewer deaths from heart disease than an equivalent group who did not sleep at all during the day.
(10) The benefit was greater for men than for women.
(11) (Whether women benefited at all was hard to estimate as there were too few deaths among them during the course of the study.)
(12) It was also greater for working men than for those who had retired.
(13) However, a number of previous studies done in the Mediterranean and in parts of Central America (where siestas remain common) have come up with conflicting results, but Dr Naska and Dr Trichopoulos argue that those studies have often been flawed.
(14) The subjects in some, for example, had survived heart attacks and may therefore have benefited more from napping than healthy individuals do.
(15) Given that all of the subjects of this new study were Greek, could the much-celebrated Mediterranean diet deserve credit, rather than the siestas?
(16) The firm answer from Dr Trichopoulos is" No" .
(17) And he is in a good position to say so, for it was he who did the pioneering research that put olive oil and a plant-based diet on the scientific map in the first place.
(18) Unlike some other siesta studies, his was controlled for diet, smoking, exercise and other relevant variables.
(19) The earlier findings about the benefits of the Mediterranean diet are confirmed, he says, and napping seems to help on top of that.
(20) Before buying a sofa for the office, however, it might be wise to consider the possibility of selection bias.
(21) Dr Trichopoulos concedes that" Type A"  personalities, whose hard-working lives may make them prone to heart attacks, are also much less likely to take naps during the day.
(22) That bias might be skewing the study's results.
(23) Even so, he advises, " Take a nap if you can."

(9) The study found that the group of adults who took siestas (defined as 30-minute naps) at least three times a week had a third fewer deaths from heart disease than an equivalent group who did not sleep at all during the day.

(14) The subjects in some, for example, had survived heart attacks and may therefore have benefited more from napping than healthy individuals do.
(15) Given that all of the subjects of this new study were Greek, could the much-celebrated Mediterranean diet deserve credit, rather than the siestas?
(16) The firm answer from Dr Trichopoulos is" No" .
(17) And he is in a good position to say so, for it was he who did the pioneering research that put olive oil and a plant-based diet on the scientific map in the first place.
(18) Unlike some other siesta studies, his was controlled for diet, smoking, exercise and other relevant variables.

a|true|college990.txt.passage:
(1) My father was a foreman of a sugar-cane plantation in Rio Piedras, Puerto Rico.
(2) My first job was to drive the oxen that ploughed the cane fields.
(3) I would walk behind an ox, guiding him with a broomstick.
(4) For $ 1 a day, I worked eight hours straight, with no food breaks.
(5) It was very tedious work, but it prepared me for life and taught me many lasting lessons.
(6) Because the plantation owners were always watching us, I had to be on time every day and work as hard as I could.
(7) I've never been late for any job since.
(8) I also learned about being respectful and faithful to the people you work for.
(9) More important, I earned my pay; it never entered my mind to say I was sick just because I didn't want to work.
(10) I was only six years old, but I was doing a man's job.
(11) Our family needed every dollar we could make because my father never earned more than $ 18 a week.
(12) Our home was a three-room wood shack with a dirty floor and no toilet.
(13) Nothing made me prouder than bringing home money to help my mother, father, two brothers and three sisters.
(14) This gave me self-esteem, one of the most important things a person can have.
(15) When I was seven, I got work at a golf course near our house.
(16) My job was to stand down the fairway and spot the balls as they landed, so the golfers could find them.
(17) Losing a ball meant you were fired, so I never missed one.
(18) Some nights I would lie in bed and dreamt of making thousands of dollars by playing golf and being able to buy a bicycle.
(19) The more I dreamed, the more I thought.
(20) Why not?
(21) I made my first golf club out of guava limb and a piece of pipe.
(22) Then I hammered an empty tin can into the shape of a ball.
(23) And finally I dug two small holes in the ground and hit the ball back and forth.
(24) I practiced with the same devotion and intensity.
(25) I learned working in the field - except now I was driving golf balls with club, not oxen with a broomstick.

You are given a passage and a statement labeled as either True or Not True based on the content of the passage. 
Your task is to identify the evidence scope level among the following categories:

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

- - -

### Example

Passage: 
(1) "Can I see my baby?" asked the happy new mother.
(2) When the baby was in her arms, she couldn't believe her eyes.
(3) The baby was born without ears.
(4) Time proved  that the baby's hearing was very good though he had no ears.
(5) He got on well with his classmates.
(6) But one day, he said to his mother, tears in his eyes, "A big boy called me a freak  ."  

Not True Statement: The boy couldn't hear without ears at first.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Understand the Statement
   - Statement: "The boy couldn't hear without ears at first."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: The boy was initially unable to hear due to being born without ears.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (3): States the baby was born without ears.
   - Sentence (4): States that his hearing was very good despite that.

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

   - The information spread across these sentences collectively supports the statement that India can better tackle adverse events in the foreign exchange market due to increased reserves.

(1) Most growing plants contain much more water than all other materials combined.
(2) C. R. Barnes has suggested that it is as proper to term the plant a water structure as to call a house composed mainly of brick a brick building.
(3) Certain it is that all essential processes of plant growth and development occur in water.
(4) The mineral elements from the soil that are usable by the plant must be dissolved in the soil solution before they can be taken into the root.
(5) They are carried to all parts of the growing plant and are built into essential plant materials while in a dissolved state.
(6) The carbon dioxide (CO2) from the air may enter the leaf as a gas but is dissolved in water in the leaf before it is combined with a part of the water to form simple sugars -- the base material from which the plant body is mainly built.
(7) Actively growing plant parts are generally 75 to 90 percent water.
(8) Structural parts of plants, such as woody stems no longer actively growing, may have much less water than growing tissues.
(9) The actual amount of water in the plant at any one time, however, is only a very small part of what passes through it during its development.
(10) The processes of photosynthesis, by which carbon dioxide and water are combined -- in the presence of chlorophyll  and with energy derived from light -- to form sugars, require that carbon dioxide from the air enter the plant.
(11) This occurs mainly in the leaves.
(12) The leaf surface is not solid but contains great numbers of minute openings, through which the carbon dioxide enters.
(13) The same structure that permits the one gas to enter the leaf, however, permits another gas -- water vapor -- to be lost from it.
(14) Since carbon dioxide is present in the air only in trace quantities (3 to 4 parts in 10,000 parts of air) and water vapor is near saturation in the air spaces within the leaf (at 80℉, saturated air would contain about 186 parts of water vapor in 10,000 parts of air), the total amount of water vapor lost is many times the carbon dioxide intake.
(15) Actually, because of wind and other factors, the loss of water in proportion to carbon dioxide intake may be even greater than the relative concentrations of the two gases.
(16) Also, not all of the carbon dioxide that enters the leaf is synthesized into carbohydrates .

You are given a passage and a statement labeled as either True or Not True based on the content of the passage. 
Your task is to identify the evidence scope level among the following categories:

1. Single-sentence comprehension (Single) applies when a single sentence in the passage contains all the information necessary to determine the truth of the statement.
2. Inter-sentence comprehension (Inter) involves integrating information spread across multiple sentences to arrive at a judgment.
3. Insufficient information recognition (Insufficient) applies when the passage does not provide enough information to determine the truth of the statement. This requires recognizing that the relevant information is missing, despite a full reading of the passage.

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.
- If the evidence sentence contains anaphoric expressions, and the referent is a central or frequently mentioned entity in the preceding context, it can be categorized as a case of single sentence comprehension.
- If the referent was only briefly mentioned in the prior sentence and is not easily identifiable, then the sentence that explicitly introduces or clarifies the referent should also be considered as evidence. In such cases, categorize it as inter-sentence comprehension.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Insufficient
```

- - -

### Example

Passage: 
(1) "Can I see my baby?" asked the happy new mother.
(2) When the baby was in her arms, she couldn't believe her eyes.
(3) The baby was born without ears.
(4) Time proved  that the baby's hearing was very good though he had no ears.
(5) He got on well with his classmates.
(6) But one day, he said to his mother, tears in his eyes, "A big boy called me a freak  ."  

Not True Statement: The boy couldn't hear without ears at first.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Understand the Statement
   - Statement: "The boy couldn't hear without ears at first."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: The boy was initially unable to hear due to being born without ears.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (3): States the baby was born without ears.
   - Sentence (4): States that his hearing was very good despite that.

3. Assess Scope of Evidence
   - Sentence (4) alone contains enough information to determine that the statement is not true.
   - It clearly references both the boy’s physical condition (no ears) and his ability (good hearing).
   - No anaphora resolution or cross-sentence inference is needed.

Answer: Single

---

### Input

Passage: 
(1) Most growing plants contain much more water than all other materials combined.
(2) C. R. Barnes has suggested that it is as proper to term the plant a water structure as to call a house composed mainly of brick a brick building.
(3) Certain it is that all essential processes of plant growth and development occur in water.
(4) The mineral elements from the soil that are usable by the plant must be dissolved in the soil solution before they can be taken into the root.
(5) They are carried to all parts of the growing plant and are built into essential plant materials while in a dissolved state.
(6) The carbon dioxide (CO2) from the air may enter the leaf as a gas but is dissolved in water in the leaf before it is combined with a part of the water to form simple sugars -- the base material from which the plant body is mainly built.
(7) Actively growing plant parts are generally 75 to 90 percent water.
(8) Structural parts of plants, such as woody stems no longer actively growing, may have much less water than growing tissues.
(9) The actual amount of water in the plant at any one time, however, is only a very small part of what passes through it during its development.
(10) The processes of photosynthesis, by which carbon dioxide and water are combined -- in the presence of chlorophyll  and with energy derived from light -- to form sugars, require that carbon dioxide from the air enter the plant.
(11) This occurs mainly in the leaves.
(12) The leaf surface is not solid but contains great numbers of minute openings, through which the carbon dioxide enters.
(13) The same structure that permits the one gas to enter the leaf, however, permits another gas -- water vapor -- to be lost from it.
(14) Since carbon dioxide is present in the air only in trace quantities (3 to 4 parts in 10,000 parts of air) and water vapor is near saturation in the air spaces within the leaf (at 80℉, saturated air would contain about 186 parts of water vapor in 10,000 parts of air), the total amount of water vapor lost is many times the carbon dioxide intake.
(15) Actually, because of wind and other factors, the loss of water in proportion to carbon dioxide intake may be even greater than the relative concentrations of the two gases.
(16) Also, not all of the carbon dioxide that enters the leaf is synthesized into carbohydrates .  

Not True Statement: The mineral elements will not be absorbed by the plant unless they are dissolved in its root.  

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. **Understand the Statement**
   - Statement: "The mineral elements will not be absorbed by the plant unless they are dissolved in its root."
   - Label: Not True
   - Key claim: Mineral elements must be dissolved in the root for absorption.

2. **Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage**
   - Sentence (4): "The mineral elements from the soil that are usable by the plant must be dissolved in the soil solution before they can be taken into the root."
   - Sentence (5): "They are carried to all parts of the growing plant and are built into essential plant materials while in a dissolved state."

3. **Assess Scope of Evidence**
   - Sentence (4) states that mineral elements must be dissolved in the soil solution before being taken into the root.
   - Sentence (5) further clarifies that these dissolved elements are then carried to all parts of the plant.
   - The statement claims that the mineral elements must be dissolved in the root, which is not explicitly stated in the passage.
   - The passage does not provide enough information to determine whether the mineral elements must be dissolved in the root specifically, or if being dissolved in the soil solution is sufficient.

Answer: Insufficient

General Guidance:
If a single sentence fully supports or refutes the factuality of a statement, check whether that evidence sentence explicitly refers to all entities mentioned in the statement.

(1) The biographer has to dance between two shaky positions with respect to the subject.
(2) Too close a relation, and the writer may be objectivity.

(6) Similarly, a king's servant might not be the best one to write a biography of that king.
(7) But a foreigner might not have the knowledge and sympathy necessary to write the king's biography-not for a readership from within the kingdom, at any rate.
(8) There is no ideal position for such a task.
(9) The biographer has to work with the position he or she has in the world, adjusting that position as necessary to deal with the subject.
(10) Every position has strengths and weaknesses: to thrive, a writer must try to become aware of these, evaluate them in terms of the subject, and select a position accordingly.
(11) When their subjects are heroes or famous figures, biographies often reveal a democratic motive: they attempt to show that their subjects are only human, no better than anyone else.

(18) A subject might have several biographies, even several "authentic" ones.
(19) We sense intuitively that no one is in a position to tell the story of a life, perhaps not even the subject, and this has been proved by the history of biography.

a|true|college578.txt.passage:
(1) Governments around the world are struggling to cope with the rising cost of health care, and of drugs in particular.
(2) Many rich countries have resorted to price controls, and some on the American left advocate them noisily.
(3) But drug firms maintain that America, where they are free to price patented pills largely as they please, is the engine of global pharmaceutical innovation, while price-controlling Europeans are free riders.
(4) That, says PhRMA, the industry's lobby based in Washington, D.C., is because price regulations seen in other rich countries" chill innovation, impede patients' access to the newest cutting-edge medicines, and trigger innovators to relocate to countries with more progressive public policy."
(5) A new study, written by Donald Light, a visiting professor at Stanford University, claims that European drug firms are more innovative than American ones, in spite of price controls.
(6) That flies in the face of an influential paper published in the same journal in 2006, which examined the geographic origins of drugs registered between 1982 and 2003 and concluded that favourable public policies had helped propel America to the top of the list.
(7) PhRMA has rushed to denounce Mr Light's study, insisting it gives a" distorted picture"  and understates the impact of" home-grown innovation" .
(8) The tiff exposes two fallacies that come from looking at a globalised industry through national spectacles.
(9) Both sides attribute new drugs to a specific country of origin (based on the location of the headquarters of the firm that first launches them).
(10) But Patricia Danzon of the Wharton Business School argues that this makes little sense when most big drug firms have laboratories in several countries and often acquire drugs under development from biotechnology firms located elsewhere.
(11) By the same token, firms develop drugs for the global market, observes Alan Garber of Stanford, not just for the local one.
(12) So the imposition of price caps in a given country will not necessarily quash innovation there, thanks to the lure of exports.
(13) The exception to this rule, of course, is the United States, which alone accounts for over 40% of global sales.
(14) So what would happen if America's Congress imposes price regulations?
(15) If the cuts are swinging, then the prize for inventors everywhere will be reduced.
(16) But short of such a radical scenario, pricing reform would not kill off innovation, according to Sanford Bernstein, a financial-research firm.
(17) It has analysed the likely impact of a cut of 20% in the prices Medicare, the government health scheme for the elderly, pays for its drugs.
(18) It concluded that the earnings per share of big pharmaceutical firms would drop by 3-8%.
(19) There is even reason to think that pricing reforms may boost innovation.
(20) Britain and Germany are pioneering comparative reviews of drugs' effectiveness and cost-benefit analyses aimed at reimbursing firms for new drugs based on how well they perform.
(21) Janssen-Cilag persuaded Britain's health service to accept Velcade, its expensive cancer drug, by offering a money-back guarantee if it did not work as well as promised.
(22) The American pharmaceutical lobby is violently opposed to making such an approach compulsory.
(23) Some suspect that its opposition stems from the fear that many expensive and profitable pills would be found to be of dubious value.
(24) But as Ms Danzon points out, " Comparative-effectiveness reviews are an indirect form of price control-but one that is consistent with encouraging innovation."
